items.title: Always evaluate on held-out data
items.avoid: Publishing training-set metrics as the headline model quality number.
items.prefer: Report validation/test metrics from predict() on rows never used in fit(); keep a locked test set for final checks.
items.why: Generalization is the production goal — unseen-data metrics are the only honest pre-deployment proxy.
items.example: Dashboard shows test F1=0.84 and train F1=0.97 side by side so stakeholders see the generalization gap explicitly.

items.title: Prevent leakage before measuring generalization
items.avoid: Fitting scalers, encoders, or feature selectors on the full dataset before splitting.
items.prefer: Split first; fit preprocessing on train only; transform validation/test with train-fitted artifacts.
items.why: Leakage makes held-out rows partially seen during training — generalization estimates become optimistically biased.

items.title: Watch the generalization gap during development
items.avoid: Chasing zero training loss while validation error climbs.
items.prefer: Track train vs validation curves; stop or regularize when the gap widens (see Early Stopping).
items.why: A widening gap signals memorization — continuing training hurts unseen-data performance.
items.example: Epoch 40: train loss 0.05, val loss 0.22 and rising — stop training and roll back to epoch 25 checkpoint.

items.title: Match evaluation splits to deployment reality
items.avoid: Random splits when production requires time-based or group-based hold-out.
items.prefer: Use temporal splits for forecasting, user/group hold-out for personalized systems, and geographic hold-out when regions deploy separately.
items.why: Generalization estimates are only meaningful when held-out data resembles how unseen production data arrives.
items.example: Train on Jan–Sep transactions, validate on Oct–Dec — mirrors scoring future months, not random row shuffles.
